input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The study focuses on a building‑scale Living Lab used as the physical twin. It is a fully instrumented research and demonstration building within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations (AECO) domain, equipped with structural, envelope, HVAC, lighting, and occupancy sensor subsystems. The Living Lab operates across the full building lifecycle—from design through commissioning, daily operation, and refurbishment—making it a representative asset for digital‑twin deployment in complex, heterogeneous infrastructure environments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers real‑time monitoring of sensor streams, physics‑based simulation of building performance, life‑cycle‑encompassing optimization of energy consumption and occupant comfort, predictive analytics for fault detection, and rich, interactive 3D visualization for stakeholders. Services are exposed to facility managers, designers, and researchers through dashboards and APIs.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation is grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM) for static asset representation, uses Eclipse Hono for secure IoT data ingestion, Eclipse Ditto for digital‑twin state management and bidirectional data exchange, and employs Unreal Engine to render high‑fidelity, interactive visualisations of the building model.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The document states that the Digital Twin provides real‑time insights by continuously integrating runtime data with static models to support predictive analytics across the building lifecycle, but it does not specify synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Geometric models are obtained from BIM representations; behavioral models consist of the control logic that Building Control Engineers define directly within the BIM environment; analytical models are not explicitly described in the document. Sensors, actuators and controllers are modeled as data entities inside BIM, providing the primary data sources for the twin. The framework applies model‑driven engineering (MDE) to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable digital‑twin artifacts. A unified semantic foundation links geometric entities to their associated sensing, actuation, and control components, forming the model relationships used by the twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are represented as model elements within the BIM environment by the Building Control Engineer. Each actuator definition includes an identifier that can be addressed by the Digital Twin runtime to issue control commands. The DT framework provides remote actuation via its real‑time data exchange layer, allowing the Facility Manager to operate the physical infrastructure through the twin. No specific hardware types or actuation ranges are detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are also defined as BIM model elements by the Building Control Engineer. The semantic enrichment captures sensor metadata (type, location) which MDE extracts to generate twin sensor models. The runtime continuously streams sensor measurements from the physical building to the twin, maintaining synchronization. The document does not enumerate sensor categories, sampling rates, or precision values.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by embedding device definitions and control logic directly into the BIM model, creating a single source of truth. Continuous real‑time synchronization between physical and digital twins functions as an implicit validation mechanism: discrepancies trigger updates. The framework relies on the unified semantic foundation and MDE‑generated artifacts to ensure consistency, but explicit validation, verification, uncertainty quantification, or quality‑assurance procedures are not described.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical‑to‑virtual interaction is realized through the framework’s real‑time data exchange layer, which continuously synchronizes sensor readings and device states from the building with the digital twin. Data are injected via the modular architecture’s standardized extension points, allowing custom services to be integrated. Runtime configuration management enables preprocessing, quality‑assurance adjustments, and pipeline reconfiguration without full redeployment, ensuring continuous synchronization, although specific data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, or event triggers are not enumerated in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Bidirectional communication is supported by the DT Framework, which relays control commands from Facility Managers (or other DT users) to physical building devices. Commands are generated from the digital twin model in Ditto and transmitted through the same Eclipse Hono gateway to the field devices, enabling real‑time monitoring and control. The document does not provide details on command formats, validation, authentication, feedback loops, or execution confirmation.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework defines a full DT lifecycle composed of (1) device modeling within BIM where sensors, actuators, and controllers are added; (2) model enrichment and transformation using model‑driven engineering (MDE) to extract a structured representation; (3) automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts; (4) deployment and runtime operation where real‑time data exchange keeps the twin synchronized; and (5) ongoing evolution through modular extension points. Transition between stages is managed by the MDE pipeline that converts enriched BIM models into executable twin components, and lifecycle governance is implied by the requirement to support the complete life‑cycle of DT deployment and operation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: DT creation follows a model‑driven deployment process. Building Control Engineers model devices and encode control logic in BIM; DT Engineers develop plugins and custom services; the MDE pipeline extracts the semantic model and generates the DT runtime. The approach relies on MDE principles for automated artifact generation and on a modular architecture with standardized extension points for adding new services. No explicit QA, testing, version‑control or evolution‑management practices are described in the document.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides decision support through continuous real‑time data exchange that keeps the physical building and its digital counterpart synchronized, and through interactive 3D visualization that allows Facility Managers to monitor system states and issue control actions. Custom services and analytics can be plugged into the modular framework, enabling advanced processing of sensor streams for operational insights. The document does not specify confidence metrics for recommendations.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The approach is built on BIM (Building Information Modeling) as the core industry standard for representing buildings and for encoding device information. BIM serves as the semantic basis from which structured DT models are derived. The framework defines standardized extension points (i.e., a modular API contract) to ensure that custom services and analytics can interoperate with the core twin platform. No additional industry standards, certification schemes, or formal compliance frameworks (e.g., IFC, ISO 19650) are mentioned in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description correctly identifies the system as a building-scale Living Lab used as a physical twin within the AECO domain, which is explicitly mentioned in the Abstract ('demonstrate the practical application of our approach in a Living Lab case study') and Introduction ('DT of a Living Lab'). The mention of full lifecycle instrumentation is supported by the Abstract ('complete building life cycle') and the reference to 'real-time monitoring' and 'simulation'. However, the specific subsystems (structural, envelope, HVAC, lighting) are not detailed in the provided text, though the general context of building infrastructure is implied.

The services are well-supported by the Abstract, which mentions 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization' and 'rich, interactive visualization.' The Introduction also references 'predictive analytics.' The description's alignment with stakeholder use is supported by 'stakeholder collaboration' in the Introduction and roles like 'Facility Manager' in Table I. However, the text does not explicitly mention APIs or specific user roles like 'designers' in the context of services, so some details are inferred rather than directly stated.

The description is highly accurate and strongly grounded in the sources. The Abstract explicitly states: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization.' It also notes that the framework is 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM).' These details are consistently reinforced in the Introduction and other sections, making the extraction complete and specific.

Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring', 'real-time data exchange', 'live synchronization'.

The extraction correctly states that this characteristic is 'Not in Document'. The source text describes a model-driven framework and a specific Living Lab case study but does not provide details on multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures of twins, or coordination mechanisms between different twin instances. It focuses on the architecture of a single DT platform and the integration of components within it, not the multiplicities of the twins themselves.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the system architecture, component integration, and orchestration. Evidence: 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto', 'layered system architecture'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly accurate and detailed, directly reflecting the source text. It correctly identifies geometric models from BIM, behavioral models as control logic defined by engineers, and the use of MDE to extract data. It also notes the absence of explicit analytical models. Supported by quotes: 'BIM enables the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that incorporate geometric... characteristics', 'defining control logic... directly within the BIM environment', 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts'.

The description is precise and well-supported by the source text. It correctly states that actuators are modeled in BIM by the engineer, includes addressing identifiers, and enables remote control. The absence of specific hardware details is also correctly noted. Supported by quotes: 'Building Control Engineer... defines... actuators... directly within the BIM environment', 'extract relevant metadata (e.g., tenant and device ID)', and 'bidirectional communication'.

The extraction is specific and accurate regarding the role of sensors in the BIM environment and their connection to the runtime. It correctly describes the semantic enrichment and real-time streaming capabilities. The note about missing specific technical specifications is accurate. Supported by quotes: 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment', 'automates the extraction of domain semantics', and 'continuous real-time synchronization'.

The extraction correctly identifies the 'unified semantic foundation' and BIM as mechanisms for consistency. However, it includes unsupported speculation by stating 'discrepancies trigger updates,' which is not in the text. Furthermore, it claims explicit validation procedures are 'not described,' whereas the source explicitly mentions using the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' to evaluate the twin. Quotes: 'unified semantic foundation', 'ensure the integrity', 'evaluate the entanglement... using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

The description aligns well with the source text, detailing the roles ('Building Control Engineers... model devices', 'DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services') and the MDE pipeline ('model-driven deployment framework', 'leverages MDE principles'). It correctly identifies the absence of detailed governance practices, as the text mentions 'validating the model' but does not elaborate on specific QA, testing, or version control strategies.

The extraction is supported by the text regarding decision support capabilities: real-time data exchange ('real-time data exchange', 'continuous synchronization'), visualization and monitoring ('interactive 3D visualization', 'Facility Managers who monitor and control'), and extensibility ('integration of custom services and analytics'). It is also accurate in stating that confidence metrics are not specified.

The extraction correctly identifies BIM as the core standard and the use of standardized extension points. It also accurately states that specific industry standards like IFC or ISO 19650 are not mentioned in the text. Supported by: 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry', and 'framework features standardized extension points'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source documents do not contain information regarding data ownership, privacy protection measures, data anonymization, or consent management. The term 'governance' is mentioned in the context of Model-driven Engineering (MDE) to describe encoding 'organizational policies and architectural decisions into formal models,' but this refers to system engineering governance rather than data privacy or ownership policies.

The provided text discusses real-time data exchange and connectivity using Eclipse Hono and Ditto, but it does not detail cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, or fail-safe mechanisms. The focus is on interoperability, scalability, and maintainability rather than security or safety implementations.
